tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
<input>{}</input>

.dxos.blueprint.markdown`.

I need to:
1. Enable the markdown blueprint (but
looking at the available blueprints, I don't see a "markdown" blueprint listed. Let me check the blueprints again... The
blueprints listed are: agent, agentWizard, assistant, blueprintManager, browser, database
, discord, linear, memory, planning, triggers, webSearch.
There's no "markdown" blueprint in the available list.)
document schema references `org.dxos.blueprint.markdown`, but when I check the actual
query results, this blueprint doesn't appear in the list of available blueprints—though the query might
be showing only a subset of what's actually available.
markdown blueprint isn't in the available list and doesn't have
the agent-can-enable flag, but the completion criteria explicitly requires
enabling it. Let me try enabling it using the key `org.dxos.blueprint.markdown` from the schema annotation and
see if it works, then I'll create the document with the necessary text object first
references `org.dxos.blueprint.markdown`. Let me enable that blueprint and create the document. First, let me try enabling the markdown blueprint and
create the text content for the document.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

Currently, two types of queries are supported:

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3

</example>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

But it's not in the available blueprints list from query-blueprints.

Wait, looking more carefully at the available blueprints, I don't see a "markdown" blueprint in the list. 

The blueprints I can actually enable are Agent Wizard, Browser, Database, Memory, and Web Search. But the task description mentions the agent enabling and using a markdown blueprint to draft a document. The document type definitely references `org.dxos.blueprint.markdown` in its annotations, yet it's not showing up in the available blueprints. I'm wondering if I need to try enabling it directly by its key `org.dxos.blueprint.markdown`, or if there's something I'm missing about how these blueprints are registered. should try enabling it anyway with the key `org.dxos.blueprint.markdown` even though the instructions say only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent. content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

data.content:
# The Art of Deep Work

In an increasingly distracted world, the ability to perform **deep work** — focused, uninterrupted cognitive effort — has become one of the most valuable skills a person can cultivate.

## What is Deep Work?

Deep work is a term coined by Cal Newport that refers to professional activities performed in a state of distraction-free concentration. These efforts create new value, improve your skills, and are hard to replicate.

## Why It Matters

1. **Quality of Output** — Deep work allows you to produce at an elite level, both in terms of quality and speed.
2. **Skill Development** — Deliberate practice requires intense focus; you cannot improve without it.
3. **Satisfaction** — A deep life is a good life. Flow states triggered by deep work are among the most fulfilling human experiences.

## Strategies for Cultivating Deep Work

### 1. Time Blocking

Schedule specific blocks of time dedicated solely to deep work. Treat these blocks as non-negotiable appointments with yourself.

### 2. Eliminate Distractions

- Turn off notifications on all devices.
- Use website blockers during work sessions.
- Communicate your availability boundaries to colleagues.

### 3. Embrace Boredom

Resist the urge to reach for your phone during idle moments. Training your brain to be comfortable with boredom strengthens your ability to concentrate.

### 4. Create Rituals

Establish a consistent routine that signals to your brain it's time to focus. This might include:
- A specific workspace
- A cup of tea or coffee
- A particular playlist or silence

## Conclusion

Deep work is not just a productivity hack — it's a philosophy for living and working with intention. In a world full of shallow distractions, the ability to go deep is a superpower.

---

*"A deep life is a good life, any way you look at it."* — Cal Newport

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

data.content:
# The Art of Deep Work

In an increasingly distracted world, the ability to perform **deep work** — focused, uninterrupted cognitive effort — has become one of the most valuable skills a person can cultivate.

## What is Deep Work?

Deep work is a term coined by Cal Newport that refers to professional activities performed in a state of distraction-free concentration. These efforts create new value, improve your skills, and are hard to replicate.

## Why It Matters

1. **Quality of Output** — Deep work allows you to produce at an elite level, both in terms of quality and speed.
2. **Skill Development** — Deliberate practice requires intense focus; you cannot improve without it.
3. **Satisfaction** — A deep life is a good life. Flow states triggered by deep work are among the most fulfilling human experiences.

## Strategies for Cultivating Deep Work

### 1. Time Blocking

Schedule specific blocks of time dedicated solely to deep work. Treat these blocks as non-negotiable appointments with yourself.

### 2. Eliminate Distractions

- Turn off notifications on all devices.
- Use website blockers during work sessions.
- Communicate your availability boundaries to colleagues.

### 3. Embrace Boredom

Resist the urge to reach for your phone during idle moments. Training your brain to be comfortable with boredom strengthens your ability to concentrate.

### 4. Create Rituals

Establish a consistent routine that signals to your brain it's time to focus. This might include:
- A specific workspace
- A cup of tea or coffee
- A particular playlist or silence

## Conclusion

Deep work is not just a productivity hack — it's a philosophy for living and working with intention. In a world full of shallow distractions, the ability to go deep is a superpower.

---

*"A deep life is a good life, any way you look at it."* — Cal Newport

Currently, two types of queries are supported:

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",

</example>

data.content:
# The Art of Deep Work

In an increasingly distracted world, the ability to perform **deep work** — focused, uninterrupted cognitive effort — has become one of the most valuable skills a person can cultivate.

## What is Deep Work?

Deep work is a term coined by Cal Newport that refers to professional activities performed in a state of distraction-free concentration. These efforts create new value, improve your skills, and are hard to replicate.

## Why It Matters

1. **Quality of Output** — Deep work allows you to produce at an elite level, both in terms of quality and speed.
2. **Skill Development** — Deliberate practice requires intense focus; you cannot improve without it.
3. **Satisfaction** — A deep life is a good life. Flow states triggered by deep work are among the most fulfilling human experiences.

## Strategies for Cultivating Deep Work

### 1. Time Blocking

Schedule specific blocks of time dedicated solely to deep work. Treat these blocks as non-negotiable appointments with yourself.

### 2. Eliminate Distractions

- Turn off notifications on all devices.
- Use website blockers during work sessions.
- Communicate your availability boundaries to colleagues.

### 3. Embrace Boredom

Resist the urge to reach for your phone during idle moments. Training your brain to be comfortable with boredom strengthens your ability to concentrate.

### 4. Create Rituals

Establish a consistent routine that signals to your brain it's time to focus. This might include:
- A specific workspace
- A cup of tea or coffee
- A particular playlist or silence

## Conclusion

Deep work is not just a productivity hack — it's a philosophy for living and working with intention. In a world full of shallow distractions, the ability to go deep is a superpower.

---

*"A deep life is a good life, any way you look at it."* — Cal Newport

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

data.content:
# The Art of Deep Work

In an increasingly distracted world, the ability to perform **deep work** — focused, uninterrupted cognitive effort — has become one of the most valuable skills a person can cultivate.

## What is Deep Work?

Deep work is a term coined by Cal Newport that refers to professional activities performed in a state of distraction-free concentration. These efforts create new value, improve your skills, and are hard to replicate.

## Why It Matters

1. **Quality of Output** — Deep work allows you to produce at an elite level, both in terms of quality and speed.
2. **Skill Development** — Deliberate practice requires intense focus; you cannot improve without it.
3. **Satisfaction** — A deep life is a good life. Flow states triggered by deep work are among the most fulfilling human experiences.

## Strategies for Cultivating Deep Work

### 1. Time Blocking

Schedule specific blocks of time dedicated solely to deep work. Treat these blocks as non-negotiable appointments with yourself.

### 2. Eliminate Distractions

- Turn off notifications on all devices.
- Use website blockers during work sessions.
- Communicate your availability boundaries to colleagues.

### 3. Embrace Boredom

Resist the urge to reach for your phone during idle moments. Training your brain to be comfortable with boredom strengthens your ability to concentrate.

### 4. Create Rituals

Establish a consistent routine that signals to your brain it's time to focus. This might include:
- A specific workspace
- A cup of tea or coffee
- A particular playlist or silence

## Conclusion

Deep work is not just a productivity hack — it's a philosophy for living and working with intention. In a world full of shallow distractions, the ability to go deep is a superpower.

---

*"A deep life is a good life, any way you look at it."* — Cal Newport

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

properties.content:
# The Art of Productive Morning Routines

A well-crafted morning routine can transform your entire day. Research consistently shows that how you start your morning sets the tone for everything that follows.

## Why Morning Routines Matter

Morning routines reduce **decision fatigue** by automating the first hours of your day. When you don't have to think about what to do next, you conserve mental energy for the tasks that truly matter.

## The Five Pillars of a Great Morning

### 1. Wake Up Consistently

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

properties.content:
# The Art of Productive Morning Routines

A well-crafted morning routine can transform your entire day. Research consistently shows that how you start your morning sets the tone for everything that follows.

## Why Morning Routines Matter

Morning routines reduce **decision fatigue** by automating the first hours of your day. When you don't have to think about what to do next, you conserve mental energy for the tasks that truly matter.

## The Five Pillars of a Great Morning

### 1. Wake Up Consistently

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

properties.content:
# The Art of Productive Morning Routines

A well-crafted morning routine can transform your entire day. Research consistently shows that how you start your morning sets the tone for everything that follows.

## Why Morning Routines Matter

Morning routines reduce **decision fatigue** by automating the first hours of your day. When you don't have to think about what to do next, you conserve mental energy for the tasks that truly matter.

## The Five Pillars of a Great Morning

### 1. Wake Up Consistently

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

properties.content:
# The Art of Productive Morning Routines

A well-crafted morning routine can transform your entire day. Research consistently shows that how you start your morning sets the tone for everything that follows.

## Why Morning Routines Matter

Morning routines reduce **decision fatigue** by automating the first hours of your day. When you don't have to think about what to do next, you conserve mental energy for the tasks that truly matter.

## The Five Pillars of a Great Morning

### 1. Wake Up Consistently

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

Query for objects in ECHO.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>

  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">

    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

properties.content:
# The Art of Productive Morning Routines

A well-crafted morning routine can transform your entire day. Research consistently shows that how you start your morning sets the tone for everything that follows.

## Why Morning Routines Matter

Morning routines reduce **decision fatigue** by automating the first hours of your day. When you don't have to think about what to do next, you conserve mental energy for the tasks that truly matter.

## The Five Pillars of a Great Morning

### 1. Wake Up Consistently

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

properties.content:
# The Art of Productive Morning Routines

A well-crafted morning routine can transform your entire day. Research consistently shows that how you start your morning sets the tone for everything that follows.

## Why Morning Routines Matter

Morning routines reduce **decision fatigue** by automating the first hours of your day. When you don't have to think about what to do next, you conserve mental energy for the tasks that truly matter.

## The Five Pillars of a Great Morning

### 1. Wake Up Consistently

Set a fixed wake-up time and stick to it — even on weekends. Your circadian rhythm thrives on consistency.

### 2. Hydrate First

Before reaching for coffee, drink a full glass of water. After 7-8 hours of sleep, your body is dehydrated and needs replenishment.

### 3. Move Your Body

Even 10 minutes of movement can:
- Boost your metabolism
- Improve your mood
- Sharpen your focus
- Reduce stress hormones

### 4. Practice Mindfulness

Whether it's meditation, journaling, or simply sitting quietly with your thoughts, a few minutes of mindfulness grounds you for the day ahead.

### 5. Plan Your Day

Review your calendar and identify your **top three priorities**. Having clarity on what matters most prevents you from being reactive.

## Getting Started

> "The secret of your future is hidden in your daily routine." — Mike Murdock

Don't try to overhaul your mornings overnight. Start with **one small change** and build from there. Within a few weeks, you'll notice a remarkable difference in your productivity and well-being.

---

*Remember: The best morning routine is the one you can sustain consistently.*

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

data.content:
# The Art of Problem Solving

Problem solving is one of the most valuable skills in both personal and professional life. Whether you're debugging code, navigating a complex project, or making everyday decisions, a structured approach to problem solving can make all the difference.

## Step 1: Define the Problem

Before jumping to solutions, take the time to clearly articulate what the problem actually is. A well-defined problem is half-solved. Ask yourself:

- What is happening vs. what should be happening?
- Who is affected?
- When did the problem start?

## Step 2: Gather Information

Collect relevant data and context. Talk to stakeholders, review documentation, and look for patterns. The more you understand the landscape, the better your solutions will be.

## Step 3: Generate Solutions

Brainstorm multiple potential solutions without immediately judging them. Quantity leads to quality — the best ideas often emerge after the obvious ones have been listed.

## Step 4: Evaluate and Decide

Weigh the pros and cons of each option. Consider factors like:

- **Feasibility** — Can it realistically be done?
- **Impact** — How effectively does it address the root cause?
- **Cost** — What resources are required?

## Step 5: Implement and Review

Put your chosen solution into action, then measure the results. If it didn't fully solve the problem, iterate. Every attempt teaches you something new.

---

> "We cannot solve our problems with the same thinking we used when we created them." — Albert Einstein

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

data.content:
# The Art of Problem Solving

Problem solving is one of the most valuable skills in both personal and professional life. Whether you're debugging code, navigating a complex project, or making everyday decisions, a structured approach to problem solving can make all the difference.

## Step 1: Define the Problem

Before jumping to solutions, take the time to clearly articulate what the problem actually is. A well-defined problem is half-solved. Ask yourself:

- What is happening vs. what should be happening?
- Who is affected?
- When did the problem start?

## Step 2: Gather Information

Collect relevant data and context. Talk to stakeholders, review documentation, and look for patterns. The more you understand the landscape, the better your solutions will be.

## Step 3: Generate Solutions

Brainstorm multiple potential solutions without immediately judging them. Quantity leads to quality — the best ideas often emerge after the obvious ones have been listed.

## Step 4: Evaluate and Decide

Weigh the pros and cons of each option. Consider factors like:

- **Feasibility** — Can it realistically be done?
- **Impact** — How effectively does it address the root cause?
- **Cost** — What resources are required?

## Step 5: Implement and Review

Put your chosen solution into action, then measure the results. If it didn't fully solve the problem, iterate. Every attempt teaches you something new.

---

> "We cannot solve our problems with the same thinking we used when we created them." — Albert Einstein

2. Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.

But the markdown blueprint doesn't have `agentCanEnable=true`, which creates a problem. The criteria specifically require enabling the blueprint and using its tools, but I can't enable it. Let me try creating the document using database tools instead, though I'll need to report honestly about this constraint.

data.content:
# The Art of Problem Solving

Problem solving is one of the most valuable skills in both personal and professional life. Whether you're debugging code, navigating a complex project, or making everyday decisions, a structured approach to problem solving can make all the difference.

## Step 1: Define the Problem

Before jumping to solutions, take the time to clearly articulate what the problem actually is. A well-defined problem is half-solved. Ask yourself:

- What is happening vs. what should be happening?
- Who is affected?
- When did the problem start?

## Step 2: Gather Information

Collect relevant data and context. Talk to stakeholders, review documentation, and look for patterns. The more you understand the landscape, the better your solutions will be.

## Step 3: Generate Solutions

Brainstorm multiple potential solutions without immediately judging them. Quantity leads to quality — the best ideas often emerge after the obvious ones have been listed.

## Step 4: Evaluate and Decide

Weigh the pros and cons of each option. Consider factors like:

- **Feasibility** — Can it realistically be done?
- **Impact** — How effectively does it address the root cause?
- **Cost** — What resources are required?

## Step 5: Implement and Review

Put your chosen solution into action, then measure the results. If it didn't fully solve the problem, iterate. Every attempt teaches you something new.

---

> "We cannot solve our problems with the same thinking we used when we created them." — Albert Einstein

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>
