tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown blueprint.
- Agent used markdown blueprint tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

params.name: The Art of Problem Solving
params.content:
# The Art of Problem Solving

## Introduction

Problem solving is one of the most valuable skills anyone can develop. Whether you're debugging code, navigating a complex project, or tackling everyday challenges, a structured approach to problem solving can make all the difference.

## The Five-Step Framework

### 1. Define the Problem

Before jumping to solutions, take time to clearly articulate the problem. Ask yourself:

- What exactly is going wrong?
- Who is affected?
- When did the issue first appear?

A well-defined problem is already half solved.

### 2. Gather Information

Collect all relevant data and context. Talk to stakeholders, review documentation, and examine the evidence. The more you understand the landscape, the better your solution will be.

### 3. Generate Ideas

Brainstorm freely without judgment. Quantity matters more than quality at this stage. Techniques like mind mapping, reverse thinking, and the "five whys" can help unlock creative solutions.

### 4. Evaluate and Choose

Weigh each idea against your criteria:

- **Feasibility** — Can it realistically be implemented?
- **Impact** — Will it fully resolve the problem?
- **Cost** — What resources are required?

Select the option that best balances these factors.

### 5. Implement and Reflect

Put your chosen solution into action, then review the results. Did it work? What would you do differently next time? This reflection loop is what turns problem solving from a one-time act into a lasting skill.

## Conclusion

Great problem solvers aren't born — they're made through practice and deliberate thinking. By following a structured framework, you can approach any challenge with confidence and clarity.

params.name: The Art of Problem Solving
params.content:
# The Art of Problem Solving

## Introduction

Problem solving is one of the most valuable skills anyone can develop. Whether you're debugging code, navigating a complex project, or tackling everyday challenges, a structured approach to problem solving can make all the difference.

## The Five-Step Framework

### 1. Define the Problem

Before jumping to solutions, take time to clearly articulate the problem. Ask yourself:

- What exactly is going wrong?
- Who is affected?
- When did the issue first appear?

A well-defined problem is already half solved.

### 2. Gather Information

Collect all relevant data and context. Talk to stakeholders, review documentation, and examine the evidence. The more you understand the landscape, the better your solution will be.

### 3. Generate Ideas

Brainstorm freely without judgment. Quantity matters more than quality at this stage. Techniques like mind mapping, reverse thinking, and the "five whys" can help unlock creative solutions.

### 4. Evaluate and Choose

Weigh each idea against your criteria:

- **Feasibility** — Can it realistically be implemented?
- **Impact** — Will it fully resolve the problem?
- **Cost** — What resources are required?

Select the option that best balances these factors.

### 5. Implement and Reflect

Put your chosen solution into action, then review the results. Did it work? What would you do differently next time? This reflection loop is what turns problem solving from a one-time act into a lasting skill.

## Conclusion

Great problem solvers aren't born — they're made through practice and deliberate thinking. By following a structured framework, you can approach any challenge with confidence and clarity.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Skills Definitions

<skill>
  You can query available skills and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-skills] to refresh the list of available skills.
  Use [enable-skills] to enable skills by their keys. Always call [query-skills] first.
  Only skills with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_skills>
  -  "Agent skill"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Skill Manager" -- Query and enable skills in the current conversation.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_skills>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the skill to use it, only then the tools from that skill will appear.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</skill>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown skill.
- Agent used markdown skill tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

params.name: The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide
params.content:
# The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide

## Introduction

Habits shape nearly **40% of our daily actions**. Understanding how they work can help us build better routines and break unwanted patterns.

## The Habit Loop

Every habit follows a three-step cycle:

1. **Cue** — A trigger that initiates the behavior.
2. **Routine** — The behavior itself, whether physical, mental, or emotional.
3. **Reward** — The benefit you gain, which reinforces the loop.

## Tips for Building Good Habits

- **Start small.** Begin with a habit so easy you can't say no. Want to exercise? Start with just five minutes.
- **Stack habits.** Attach a new habit to an existing one. For example: *"After I pour my morning coffee, I will write in my journal for two minutes."*
- **Track your progress.** A simple checklist or calendar can provide visual motivation.
- **Be patient.** Research suggests it takes an average of **66 days** for a new behavior to become automatic.

## Breaking Bad Habits

- Identify the **cue** that triggers the unwanted behavior.
- Replace the **routine** with a healthier alternative.
- Keep the same **reward** to satisfy the underlying craving.

## Conclusion

Small, consistent changes compound over time into remarkable results. Start with one habit today, and let momentum do the rest.

---

*"We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit."* — Will Durant

Query for objects in ECHO.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:

  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {

    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.name: The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide
params.content:
# The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide

## Introduction

Habits shape nearly **40% of our daily actions**. Understanding how they work can help us build better routines and break unwanted patterns.

## The Habit Loop

Every habit follows a three-step cycle:

1. **Cue** — A trigger that initiates the behavior.
2. **Routine** — The behavior itself, whether physical, mental, or emotional.
3. **Reward** — The benefit you gain, which reinforces the loop.

## Tips for Building Good Habits

- **Start small.** Begin with a habit so easy you can't say no. Want to exercise? Start with just five minutes.
- **Stack habits.** Attach a new habit to an existing one. For example: *"After I pour my morning coffee, I will write in my journal for two minutes."*
- **Track your progress.** A simple checklist or calendar can provide visual motivation.
- **Be patient.** Research suggests it takes an average of **66 days** for a new behavior to become automatic.

## Breaking Bad Habits

- Identify the **cue** that triggers the unwanted behavior.
- Replace the **routine** with a healthier alternative.
- Keep the same **reward** to satisfy the underlying craving.

## Conclusion

Small, consistent changes compound over time into remarkable results. Start with one habit today, and let momentum do the rest.

---

*"We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit."* — Will Durant

content.text: The document has been successfully created. Let me verify it exists by opening it.

params.name: The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide
params.content:
# The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide

## Introduction

Habits shape nearly **40% of our daily actions**. Understanding how they work can help us build better routines and break unwanted patterns.

## The Habit Loop

Every habit follows a three-step cycle:

1. **Cue** — A trigger that initiates the behavior.
2. **Routine** — The behavior itself, whether physical, mental, or emotional.
3. **Reward** — The benefit you gain, which reinforces the loop.

## Tips for Building Good Habits

- **Start small.** Begin with a habit so easy you can't say no. Want to exercise? Start with just five minutes.
- **Stack habits.** Attach a new habit to an existing one. For example: *"After I pour my morning coffee, I will write in my journal for two minutes."*
- **Track your progress.** A simple checklist or calendar can provide visual motivation.
- **Be patient.** Research suggests it takes an average of **66 days** for a new behavior to become automatic.

## Breaking Bad Habits

- Identify the **cue** that triggers the unwanted behavior.
- Replace the **routine** with a healthier alternative.
- Keep the same **reward** to satisfy the underlying craving.

## Conclusion

Small, consistent changes compound over time into remarkable results. Start with one habit today, and let momentum do the rest.

---

*"We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit."* — Will Durant

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.name: The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide
params.content:
# The Power of Habits: A Quick Guide

## Introduction

Habits shape nearly **40% of our daily actions**. Understanding how they work can help us build better routines and break unwanted patterns.

## The Habit Loop

Every habit follows a three-step cycle:

1. **Cue** — A trigger that initiates the behavior.
2. **Routine** — The behavior itself, whether physical, mental, or emotional.
3. **Reward** — The benefit you gain, which reinforces the loop.

## Tips for Building Good Habits

- **Start small.** Begin with a habit so easy you can't say no. Want to exercise? Start with just five minutes.
- **Stack habits.** Attach a new habit to an existing one. For example: *"After I pour my morning coffee, I will write in my journal for two minutes."*
- **Track your progress.** A simple checklist or calendar can provide visual motivation.
- **Be patient.** Research suggests it takes an average of **66 days** for a new behavior to become automatic.

## Breaking Bad Habits

- Identify the **cue** that triggers the unwanted behavior.
- Replace the **routine** with a healthier alternative.
- Keep the same **reward** to satisfy the underlying craving.

## Conclusion

Small, consistent changes compound over time into remarkable results. Start with one habit today, and let momentum do the rest.

---

*"We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit."* — Will Durant

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.name: The Art of Simplicity
params.content:
# The Art of Simplicity

## Why Less is More

In a world overflowing with information, complexity, and constant noise, **simplicity** stands as a quiet superpower. From design to communication to daily habits, embracing simplicity can transform how we think, work, and live.

## Three Principles of Simplicity

### 1. Clarity Over Cleverness

The best ideas are ones that anyone can understand. Whether you're writing code, crafting a message, or solving a problem — aim for clarity first.

> "Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication." — Leonardo da Vinci

### 2. Focus on What Matters

Not everything deserves your attention. By identifying what truly matters and letting go of the rest, you create space for meaningful work.

- Prioritize outcomes over activity.
- Say no more often than you say yes.
- Measure progress, not busyness.

### 3. Iterate and Refine

Simplicity is rarely achieved on the first attempt. It emerges through cycles of creation, reflection, and refinement.

1. Start with a rough draft.
2. Remove anything that doesn't serve the core purpose.
3. Polish what remains until it feels effortless.

## Conclusion

Simplicity isn't about doing less for its own sake — it's about making room for what truly counts. Whether you're designing a product, writing a document, or planning your week, the pursuit of simplicity is always worthwhile.

params.name: The Art of Simplicity
params.content:
# The Art of Simplicity

## Why Less is More

In a world overflowing with information, complexity, and constant noise, **simplicity** stands as a quiet superpower. From design to communication to daily habits, embracing simplicity can transform how we think, work, and live.

## Three Principles of Simplicity

### 1. Clarity Over Cleverness

The best ideas are ones that anyone can understand. Whether you're writing code, crafting a message, or solving a problem — aim for clarity first.

> "Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication." — Leonardo da Vinci

### 2. Focus on What Matters

Not everything deserves your attention. By identifying what truly matters and letting go of the rest, you create space for meaningful work.

- Prioritize outcomes over activity.
- Say no more often than you say yes.
- Measure progress, not busyness.

### 3. Iterate and Refine

Simplicity is rarely achieved on the first attempt. It emerges through cycles of creation, reflection, and refinement.

1. Start with a rough draft.
2. Remove anything that doesn't serve the core purpose.
3. Polish what remains until it feels effortless.

## Conclusion

Simplicity isn't about doing less for its own sake — it's about making room for what truly counts. Whether you're designing a product, writing a document, or planning your week, the pursuit of simplicity is always worthwhile.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

  -  "Agent skill"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"

  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Skill Manager" -- Query and enable skills in the current conversation.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]

</skill>

Query for objects in ECHO.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

The markdown document has been created successfully. Let me verify it by opening it.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Skills Definitions

<skill>
  You can query available skills and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-skills] to refresh the list of available skills.
  Use [enable-skills] to enable skills by their keys. Always call [query-skills] first.
  Only skills with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_skills>
  -  "Agent skill"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Skill Manager" -- Query and enable skills in the current conversation.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_skills>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the skill to use it, only then the tools from that skill will appear.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</skill>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown skill.
- Agent used markdown skill tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

content.text: The document has been created successfully. Let me now complete the job with the criteria met.
The markdown document has been created successfully. Let me verify it by opening it.

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Skills Definitions

<skill>
  You can query available skills and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-skills] to refresh the list of available skills.
  Use [enable-skills] to enable skills by their keys. Always call [query-skills] first.
  Only skills with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_skills>
  -  "Agent skill"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Skill Manager" -- Query and enable skills in the current conversation.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_skills>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the skill to use it, only then the tools from that skill will appear.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</skill>

Draft a new document.
Pick a title and content yourself.

Completion criteria:
- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown skill.
- Agent used markdown skill tools to draft a document.
<input>{}</input>

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

params.name: The Power of Curiosity
params.content:
# The Power of Curiosity

Curiosity is one of the most powerful forces driving human progress. From the earliest cave paintings to modern space exploration, our desire to understand the world around us has shaped civilization.

## Why Curiosity Matters

- **Innovation**: Every great invention began with a simple question — *"What if?"*
- **Learning**: Curious minds absorb knowledge faster and retain it longer.
- **Resilience**: People who stay curious tend to adapt better to change.

## Cultivating Curiosity

1. **Ask more questions.** Don't settle for surface-level answers.
2. **Explore new domains.** Read outside your usual interests.
3. **Embrace uncertainty.** The unknown is where discovery lives.

> "The important thing is not to stop questioning. Curiosity has its own reason for existing." — Albert Einstein

## Conclusion

In a world of constant change, curiosity is not just a trait — it's a superpower. Nurture it, and it will lead you to places you never imagined.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

<output_format>

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

  Only skills with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_skills>
  -  "Agent skill"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Calendar"
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox (Send)"
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Markdown" -- Work with markdown documents. Preferred over raw database operations. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Skill Manager" -- Query and enable skills in the current conversation.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_skills>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the skill to use it, only then the tools from that skill will appear.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</skill>

<skill>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</skill>

Pick a title and content yourself.

- A Markdown document object exists in the database.
- Agent enabled the markdown skill.
- Agent used markdown skill tools to draft a document.

params.name: The Power of Small Habits
params.content:
# The Power of Small Habits

## Introduction

Great achievements rarely come from a single monumental effort. Instead, they are the result of **small, consistent habits** practiced over time. Whether you want to improve your health, learn a new skill, or boost your productivity, the secret lies in the tiny actions you repeat every day.

## Why Small Habits Work

1. **Low Resistance** — Small habits are easy to start. When the barrier to entry is low, you're far more likely to follow through.
2. **Compound Growth** — Just like compound interest, small improvements accumulate. A 1% improvement each day leads to a 37x improvement over a year.
3. **Identity Shift** — Every time you perform a small habit, you cast a vote for the type of person you want to become.

## How to Build Small Habits

- **Start tiny.** Instead of "exercise for an hour," begin with "do five push-ups."
- **Stack habits.** Attach a new habit to an existing one. For example: *After I pour my morning coffee, I will write one paragraph in my journal.*
- **Track your progress.** Use a simple checklist or calendar to mark each day you follow through.
- **Celebrate small wins.** Acknowledge your consistency — it reinforces the behavior loop.

## Conclusion

You don't need to overhaul your life overnight. Start with one small habit today, stay consistent, and watch as those tiny steps carry you toward remarkable results.

> "We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit." — Will Durant

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.
